title: The Interconnect Is the Computer: Why Nvidia’s NVLink Fusion is the most strategic Trojan Horse
text:
The Interconnect Is the Computer: Why Nvidia’s NVLink Fusion is the most strategic Trojan Horse
For decades, the connector was an afterthought, a plastic lane where peripherals lived. But in the age of AI, the connector has become the computer. The recent announcement that Amazon Web Services (AWS) will integrate its custom Trainium4 chips into NVIDIA’s NVLink Fusion ecosystem is not just a partnership update. It is the final validation of a strategy NVIDIA began nearly a decade ago: the realization that the network is more sticky than the core.
To understand why Amazon, arguably NVIDIA’s biggest rival in custom silicon, would plug into NVIDIA’s matrix, we have to look at the history of how we got here.
          
        
Era 1: PCIe: The Universal Highway
For decades, PCIe (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) was the gold standard. Open, vendor-neutral, and flexible, it connected CPUs, GPUs, NICs, and SSDs through a common language. You can take a NVIDIA PCIe GPU and slide it into a vertical slot on the motherboard and scale performance generation after generation.
But PCIe was designed as an I/O fabric, not a high-frequency synchronization fabric. Its topology is fundamentally CPU-centric. Devices hang off root complexes and PCIe switches, sharing arbitration, buffers, and scheduling. Even with peer-to-peer and DMA, GPU-to-GPU traffic still contends for shared resources, introducing latency variation and jitter.
For storage and networking, this is fine. For tightly synchronized GPU collectives, PCIe becomes the bottleneck. It's bandwidth scaled (for example, PCIe Gen5 delivers ~128 GB/s bidirectional on a x16 link) but determinism didn’t. Power didn’t either. Vertical cards, airflow constraints, and form-factor limits made it increasingly difficult to feed accelerators pushing beyond 400W. PCIe didn’t fail. It simply reached the edge of what it was designed to do.
          
        
Era 2: The Specialist Era (Enter SXM)
As AI workloads shifted toward massive parallel compute, NVIDIA made a decisive move and created SXM (Server Express Module). Instead of treating the GPU as a peripheral, they promoted it to a first-class system component. An SXM is a flat, hexagonal mezzanine module that bolts directly onto a massive motherboard called the HGX Baseboard.
By mounting GPUs flat using a onto the HGX baseboard, NVIDIA unlocked radically higher power envelopes (1,000W+ for Blackwell) and aggressive cooling. Crucially, NVIDIA's proprietary NVLink and NVSwitch bypassed the CPU entirely, creating a dedicated, deterministic, GPU-native fabric.
This changed everything. GPU-to-GPU communication no longer competed with I/O traffic. Synchronization became predictable. Memory pooling became practical. Compared to PCIe, NVLink delivered orders-of-magnitude higher aggregate bandwidth, higher power density, and most importantly deterministic behavior.
NVIDIA still ships PCIe GPUs because the use cases differ. PCIe GPUs fit standard servers, integrate easily with CPUs and custom accelerators, and dominate inference. SXM GPUs are purpose-built for training, where tight coupling and synchronization matter more than modularity.
Two paths. Two philosophies. Both valid.
          
        
Era 3: The Outsiders: Amazon Trainium builds a parallel universe
Amazon internalized it all. Trainium isn’t a PCIe accelerator. It’s a system-level design. Custom form factors, custom interconnect (NeuronLink), and custom racks built around AWS’s Nitro architecture. Like NVLink, NeuronLink allows accelerators to bypass the CPU and synchronize directly. Like HGX, Amazon’s racks are vertically integrated for their workloads.
But the two worlds didn’t speak the same language. When Trainium clusters needed to interact with NVIDIA clusters, they had to fall back to standard networking (eg., EFA/RoCE or similar scale-out), bridging two high-performance islands but at slower speed. It worked. But it wasn’t elegant.
Era 4: NVLink Fusion: The Pivot
A major strategic pivot in the AI hardware landscape was announced at AWS re:Invent in December 2025. With NVLink Fusion, Nvidia opens its rack-scale fabric (built on MGX architecture and NVLink scale-up) to third-party accelerators. Hyperscalers and custom ASIC designers can now license NVLink Fusion IP to integrate their chips directly into NVIDIA's ecosystem without requiring full Nvidia GPUs.
This is not a product tweak. It’s a strategic inversion. It's not just "unbundling the GPU"; it's enabling custom XPUs/CPUs to join the NVLink domain for coherent scale-up.
(Note, Trainium4 is in design phase; Trainium3 is the current GA chip)
          
        
What NVLink Fusion really is
NVLink Fusion is Nvidia’s new business model; unbundling the GPU from the network to let custom ASICs like Trainium4 talk natively to the Nvidia networking stack. AWS can now place Trainium4 into an Nvidia's MGX-based system, integrate NVLink-compatible IP, and use Nvidia’s proven switch trays and topologies to solve scale-up communication, something that typically takes years to perfect.
From the outside, a Trainium4 based server/rack may look indistinguishable from a GB200 rack. Inside, the compute differs. The fabric does not.
          
        
Recommended by LinkedIn
Why NVIDIA would do this
Because the fabric is the leverage point. Even when customers buy non-Nvidia accelerators, Nvidia remains embedded in the highest-margin, hardest-to-replace layers: the switch trays, the rack architecture, and the scale-out networking. This isn’t about exclusivity. It’s about ecosystem gravity
          
        
Personal Perspective: The 3D Chess Board
I want to pause the technical deep dive for a moment and add some personal perspective.
As discussed in my earlier article, today’s CEOs and leadership teams are operating in an environment where the ability to learn, adapt, and pivot quickly is becoming a defining factor for long-term relevance. Most companies today are effectively playing 3D chess, running multiple strategies in parallel. They lead where possible, collaborate where necessary, and constantly reassess their position as the industry shifts.
The following section reflects how Amazon appears to be navigating this landscape, with NVLink Fusion serving as one visible example, and why I find this approach particularly compelling.
          
        
Why would AWS do this?
SemiAnalysis has published a detailed deep dive on this topic, which is worth reading in full. I’ll summarize some of their core observations here and layer in my own perspective on what this signals strategically.
At a high level, Amazon’s guiding principle for custom silicon does not appear to be winning benchmark wars or outperforming NVIDIA on headline metrics like peak FLOPS or raw memory bandwidth per chip. Their consistent North Star has been Total Cost of Ownership (TCO). In other words, performance matters, but only in the context of what it costs to deploy, scale, and operate infrastructure at massive volumes.
For most commercial AI workloads, the absolute fastest accelerator is often less important than one that is cost-effective, predictable, and good enough to meet latency or throughput requirements within an acceptable time window. Trainium fits this niche. Rather than targeting the extreme high end, it is designed to deliver competitive economics at scale.
In that sense, Trainium resembles an “Amazon Basics” approach to AI accelerators: reliable, cost-optimized, and built for mass adoption rather than luxury performance leadership. That philosophy lowers the barrier for customers to experiment and deploy without forcing an all-or-nothing commitment to a single ecosystem.
          
        
Amazon’s “Multiple Path” strategy
Amazon is deliberately avoiding a single-bet strategy. Instead, they are pursuing multiple approaches in parallel.
          
      
        
    
Why NVLink Fusion makes strategic sense for Amazon
          
      
        
    
You can visualize Nvlink Fusion at 0:49 seconds in the video below
A Note on Strategy:
If you read my previous article The End of Best-of-Breed: Why Rubin Forces a Whiteboard Reset, this might seem counter-intuitive. In that piece, I argued that Nvidia is driving toward extreme tight coupling fusing the GPU, DPU, and Network. So, why would they suddenly open the doors to custom silicon?
The answer is simple: Strategy.
Nvidia’s tight coupling wasn't about locking competitors out; it was about locking the value into the infrastructure. By forcing tight coupling of the rack and fabric, Nvidia made the network the indispensable layer. Now, they are proving it by allowing custom silicon to plug into that very same ecosystem.
The "Rubin" shift created the road; "NVLink Fusion" is the toll booth.
The future of AI hardware isn't just about who has the fastest matrix multiplication core. It’s about who owns the fabric that ties them together. With NVLink Fusion, Nvidia has signaled that they are no longer just a chip company. They are the civil engineers of the AI age and even their competitors might soon be driving on their roads.
As we watch other custom silicon efforts mature, from hyperscalers to startups, the big question for 2026 is no longer "Build vs. Buy." It is "Build Alone vs. Build Inside the Standard." The industry now has to decide if the speed of the "Walled Garden" is worth the price of admission.
PS: The opinions expressed here are my own and do not reflect the views of my current or any past employers.
Great article, I really enjoyed it. This move does make a lot of sense and I think it was mostly inevitable as more companies begin to make their own custom silicon that directly compete against NVIDIA GPUs. It makes much better business sense for NVIDIA to put into play as much of their IP as possible and to have as many customers as possible. This gives them strategic leverage to shape how fabrics in this space are designed. If you don’t control your narrative, someone else will.
